1:
The witnesses reconstruct the scene with a historian's care and divide only on small points of identity and motive. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the ground first. Keil and Delitzsch explain that, after Jonathan's warning, nothing remained for David but immediate flight; he could not return to the prophets at Ramah, and so turned to Nob to inquire the will of God through Ahimelech and to obtain bread and a weapon under the pretext of a secret royal errand (1 Samuel 22:10; 22:15) — a visit that would later draw down Saul's fearful vengeance on the priests. Ellicott and Gill place Nob between Anathoth and Jerusalem, an hour's ride from the city, a “little colony of priests,” Ellicott quoting Stanley on the eighty-five who ministered there in white linen round the last relic of the ancient tabernacle, while Clarke notes it was a city some twelve miles off.

On why David came, the schools converge and enrich one another. Ellicott lists three purposes — to take counsel with the old high priest, to provision and arm himself, and to inquire of the divine oracle by the Urim and Thummim. The Puritan Matthew Henry deepens the last two into a portrait of the exile's heart: Samuel could not protect him and Jonathan could not, so he has recourse to Ahimelech, coming partly to take an affecting farewell of the tabernacle he may never see again, and partly to enquire of the Lord — a purpose confirmed at 1 Samuel 22:15. Henry draws the comfort the whole passage turns on: it is a great mercy in a day of trouble “that we have a God to go to, to whom we may open our case.”

The one genuine dispute is over Ahimelech's identity. Gill and Wesley take him for the brother and successor of the now-dead Ahiah of 1 Samuel 14:3, both sons of Ahitub; Ellicott judges him “probably identical with Ahiah,” though he grants the point uncertain and records Payne Smith's view that Ahiah was a younger brother who served Saul at the camp. All agree, however, on the pathos of the priest's greeting. Ahimelech “was afraid,” and asked, “Why art thou alone?” The Wesleyan Clarke supposes he knew nothing of the breach with Saul and merely wondered to see the king's son-in-law unattended; Ellicott hears the “cold welcome” of a cautious, courtly man; Henry and Wesley read a sharper lesson in it — the darling suddenly advanced from a shepherd's solitude to the camp is as suddenly reduced to an exile “alone like a sparrow on the housetop,” so uncertain are the world's smiles, and those courted today may be deserted tomorrow.
2:
Here the narrative records David's falsehood, and the witnesses close ranks in condemning it while dividing on how to weigh and explain it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay out its substance — David told Ahimelech the king had charged him with a business so secret that none must know it, and that his servants were appointed to a certain place — and asked five loaves because he had spoken of attendants and wanted provision for two or three days. The Wesleyan voices are blunt. Wesley calls it “a plain lie extorted from him by fear,” pernicious to all the priests, and points to Psalm 119:29 as the place where David afterward begs God to remove the way of lying from him. Clarke is sharpest of all, denying that any of this was dictated by the Spirit of the Lord, “for there is no reason to believe that David was under the influence of Divine inspiration at this time.” He grants that antiquity held it no crime to lie to save life — quoting the pagan Diphilus, who reckoned a saving falsehood no hard thing — but answers that “a heathen may say or sing thus; but no Christian can act thus, and save his soul, though he by doing so may save his life.”

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets the fall in its human frame without softening it. Overwhelmed by his sudden ruin — home, wife, friends, and rank all torn away in a day — David the proscribed exile lies to Ahimelech; “these things must plead as his excuse,” yet they cannot acquit him. Ellicott's deeper interest is in the honesty of Scripture itself, and he presses the point with Lange: “the Holy Spirit is become the chronicler of men's foolish, yea, sinful actions,” having narrated the lies of Abraham, the incest of Lot, the simulation of the man after God's own heart.

Where the witnesses find a grain of truth is in the clause “I have appointed my servants unto such and such a place.” Ellicott judges this part strictly true — a man of David's rank would hardly go into exile with no companions, and those alluded to here probably joined him soon after his parting with Jonathan; our Lord himself speaks in Mark 2:25 of the shewbread given to David “and to those that were with him.” So the witnesses hold two things at once: the lie is real and indefensible, the cause of the priests' later slaughter which David would remember with bitter regret; and yet the record of it is the very proof of a Scripture that “could not lie, but fearlessly tells the repulsive truth” about the favourite hero of Israel. The lesson they draw is the frailty even of great faith, and the mercy of a God who neither hides his servants' sins nor abandons them in their fall.
3:
With David's request for bread the witnesses have less to weigh, and they draw the verse's meaning from its plain necessity. Gill glosses the question simply: David asks what food the priest has to hand in the house of God. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the number — David “asked for five loaves, because he had spoken of several attendants, and probably wanted to make provision for two or three days.” The detail matters, for it holds together the pretext of v. 2 and the hunger that is real beneath it; the fugitive who invents a royal errand is nonetheless a hungry man with hungry men to feed.

The Puritan Matthew Henry supplies the social background that makes the appeal intelligible. Travelling was then troublesome, “when men generally carried their provisions with them in kind, having little money and no public houses,” so that even David, lately the king's son-in-law, is reduced to begging bread by the way. Henry hears in this an echo of the Psalmist's testimony that he had never seen the righteous forsaken nor his seed begging bread (Psalm 37:25), and notes with care that David had “known the seed of the righteous begging bread occasionally, but not constantly.” The want is severe but not final; the God who feeds the ravens does not forsake his anointed even in exile.

Beneath the bare request the schools together read a study in providence and in humility. The man designated for the throne must ask a country priest for five loaves; the anointed of the Lord, who will one day feast in his own house, now depends on whatever “is present” at the tabernacle. The witnesses do not moralize the verse beyond what it will bear, but they let its very smallness speak — greatness in the purpose of God is here clothed in the poverty of a hunted man, and the bread he asks becomes, in the verses that follow, the occasion of one of Scripture's great lessons on mercy and the law. The plea turns the priest's scruple in v. 4 into a doctrine our Lord himself would draw from this very tabernacle.
4:
Now the priest's scruple opens onto the chapter's great theme, and the witnesses read it as the seed of a doctrine our Lord would later harvest. The grammatical-historical commentators set the facts. Keil and Delitzsch explain that Ahimelech had no common bread, only the holy shewbread that had been removed, which by law none but priests might eat and only in a sacred place; yet he is willing, in such a case of necessity, to set aside the Levitical rule “for the sake of observing the higher commandment of love to a neighbour” (Leviticus 19:18), asking only that the young men be clean of defilement (Leviticus 15:18). Ellicott fills in the shewbread's rule — twelve loaves, one for each tribe, laid fresh before the Presence every Sabbath, lawful food for the priests alone — and infers from Ahimelech's easy compliance that the regulation had in practice been relaxed, the stale loaves often carried home and, on urgent occasions, even given to the laity, the only proviso being ceremonial purity.

Ellicott pauses, too, on a detail the others pass over: the “pitiable” condition of the priesthood in these last days of Saul. That the ministers of the principal sanctuary of Israel had “positively no bread but the stale bread removed from before the Presence” tells its own tale of how little they were esteemed — a neglect that makes the coming massacre of Nob the more terrible and the more coldly received. Gill adds the note of haste: there was common bread to be had, but not there and not now, for David dared not linger with Doeg watching.

The abiding weight of the verse, for every school, lies in what Christ made of it. Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, and Wesley all turn to the Gospel, where our Lord cites this very incident to justify his disciples' plucking grain on the Sabbath (Matthew 12:3; Mark 2:25–26). Wesley names the “double impediment” Ahimelech must overcome — the bread's own sacredness and the required abstinence from women — and shows the priest satisfied on both by David's necessity. So the witnesses land where the Son of David lands: the ritual law, holy as it is, must yield to the higher law of mercy and human need, and a hungry man may lawfully eat the holy bread. Ahimelech, in his very hesitation and his yielding, becomes an unwitting expositor of the principle that mercy is to be preferred to sacrifice.
5:
David's reply reassures the priest, and the witnesses spend their care on a famously difficult sentence. The grammatical-historical commentators divide the verse into two claims. On the first — that women have been kept from the young men — the schools agree readily. Keil and Delitzsch render it, “women have been kept from us since yesterday and the day before,” and Wesley notes the period answers to the three days the law required at Sinai (Exodus 19:15), David and his men having hidden themselves so long from Saul. On the meaning of “the vessels of the young men are holy,” the readings part: Ellicott takes the vessels for their clothes and light baggage — “answering to the modern knapsack,” the Vulgate's vasa — while Wesley allows either that sense or “their bodies,” and records that the Septuagint, by a slight change of letters, reads simply “all the young men.”

The real crux is the last clause, “the bread is in a manner common, yea, though it were sanctified this day in the vessel.” Ellicott is refreshingly frank that “the original is here very difficult, almost utterly obscure,” and that the English Version of it “is simply meaningless.” He lays out the two best attempts. Keil and Delitzsch, with von Gerlach, read it of David's supposed royal mission — though the enterprise be in itself an “unholy way,” it becomes holy through the instrument, the ambassador of the Lord's anointed. Lange and Thenius, contrariwise, make it a remark meant to move the priest to give the bread: “though it is an unholy procedure to take the shewbread, yet it is sanctified through the instrument,” whether that instrument be David the appointed messenger or Ahimelech the sacred person of the high priest.

For all the obscurity of the words, the witnesses agree on their drift. Wesley catches it in plain speech: “considering the time, and our necessity, this may be used in a manner like common bread”; the loaf that while it stands before the Lord is too holy for the priest himself to eat becomes, once removed, food he and his household lawfully share — and so, in a case of need, may these. Ellicott sets the whole exchange beside Leviticus 24:9, and even hears an echo of it in the Church of England's rubric on the reverent consuming of what remains of the consecrated elements. The debate over the syntax is real, but the theology is settled: necessity and charity may lift what was set apart, “because God will have mercy preferred before sacrifice.”
6:
With the priest's compliance the witnesses gather the lesson the whole episode has been moving toward. Gill records the plain fact — Ahimelech, “satisfied with the account he gave,” handed over the holy bread, “acts of mercy being to be preferred to ritual services.” Whether the priest gave the full five loaves David asked, Gill observes, “is not said”; but the reason only this bread was available is plain, for it was all the sanctuary then held. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch specify what was given: the shew-loaves “taken from before Jehovah,” which had lain seven days upon the holy table (Leviticus 24:6–9) before being replaced with hot bread. The exchange is exact, lawful within the tabernacle's own economy, and yet stretched here to cover a layman's hunger.

The Wesleyan Clarke draws out the doctrine the New Testament attaches to the moment: “to this history our Lord alludes” in Mark 2:25, teaching that “in cases of absolute necessity a breach of the ritual law was no sin.” It was lawful for the priests only to eat the shewbread; but David and his companions were starving, no other bread could be had, and so “he and his companions ate of it without sin.” The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices concur: the ceremony bows to the man, the sign to the substance it was made to serve, and the God who ordained the shewbread is the same who prefers mercy to sacrifice.

Yet the verse does not close on comfort alone. Keil and Delitzsch mark how the narrator, with the loaves scarcely handed over, drops in the ominous parenthesis of the next verse — that one of Saul's servants was there that day, “detained before Jehovah.” The very scene of mercy is shadowed by a watching enemy; the bread that saves David will help to doom the priests who gave it. The witnesses hold the two together without flinching: here is the tenderness of a law that yields to need, and there, in the corner of the sanctuary, is the informer whose report will turn Nob into a place of slaughter. The mercy is real, and so is the peril; and the God who feeds his exile in the house of bread does not spare that house the sword.
7:
The narrator's parenthesis introduces the villain of the sequel, and the witnesses read Doeg with a mixture of historical precision and foreboding. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the man. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott render the Hebrew “the chiefest of the herdmen that belonged to Saul,” Ellicott calling him the keeper of the royal beasts and “chief of the household slaves,” the constable of the king — and, strikingly, “the first instance of a foreigner employed in a high function in Israel,” an Edomite raised to power at Saul's court. Both note that the Septuagint read instead “feeding the mules of Saul,” though Ellicott objects that mules do not appear among Israel's princes until David's day (2 Samuel 13:29). Wesley adds that Doeg was “an Edomite by birth, but proselyted to the Jewish religion,” retaining, beneath his borrowed faith, the old hereditary enmity of Edom to Israel.

The phrase “detained before the LORD” draws the witnesses' closest attention, and here they offer several readings without settling on one. Ellicott lists three: that Doeg was at the sanctuary as a proselyte seeking admission to Israel's communion; that he was detained for purification, whether from supposed leprosy (Leviticus 13:4) or under a Nazarite vow; or, following Ephrem Syrus, that he had committed some trespass and must remain until he had offered the appointed sacrifice. Wesley judges he was kept there “not by force but by his choice,” fixing his abode for the day, whether because it was the Sabbath or for the discharge of a vow. Clarke agrees he was “probably fulfilling some vow to the Lord,” and points forward without mercy to what the man would do — betray David's secret and so cause Saul to destroy the city and slay eighty-five priests (1 Samuel 22:9), the deed the title of Psalm 52 commemorates.

The witnesses do not so much debate the point as heap up the irony of it. The place of God's presence holds at once the hunted David seeking counsel and the treacherous Doeg watching to inform; the sanctuary “could never secure such wolves in sheep's clothing.” That a man might be “before the Lord” in body — purifying, vowing, worshipping — and yet be plotting murder in his heart is the verse's abiding warning. Ellicott records that the moment David recognized this most unscrupulous of Saul's servants, “his mind must have misgiven him,” and he hastened to arm himself and be gone. The informer's shadow falls across the whole remainder of the story, and the reader is quietly told, long before the blow, whose eyes were watching in the house of God.
8:
David's request for a weapon is brief in the telling, and the witnesses read it as the quiet setting-up of the chapter's most poignant token. Gill and Keil and Delitzsch record the plain exchange: David asks Ahimelech whether there is “spear or sword” at hand, pleading that he has brought neither his sword nor his other weapons “because the king's business required haste” — a pretext, Keil and Delitzsch note, that presses the same fiction of urgency David had used for the bread, the Hebrew word rendered “required haste” meaning literally something pressed or compressed.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott alone lingers, and turns the verse into a small drama of memory. He guesses at once “to what David pointed when he made his request” — the famous sword of Goliath, “the trophy of the combat which had for ever made his name illustrious.” In the first flush of gratitude, Ellicott supposes, David had presented that very sword to the sanctuary “as an offering to be kept for ever, a memorial of the victory of Israel over the uncircumcised” (1 Samuel 17:51); and now, “in his hour of need and humiliation,” needing every credential of his power to lead men, “he trusts the priest will let him have his glorious prize back again.” So evidently the priest understood him, for Ahimelech at once suggests restoring the treasured blade, the one war-weapon the house of God possessed.

The witnesses raise no quarrel here but let the pathos speak. The man who had once laid his victory at God's altar is reduced to asking it back to defend his own life; the champion of Israel goes out unarmed and must be armed from the very relic of his greatest deed. The verse prepares, with a novelist's economy, for the recognition of the next — that the sanctuary keeps “no other save that here,” and that the one sword left to David is the sword by which God had once made him famous. The pretext of haste is thin, and the witnesses feel it; but beneath the untruth runs a truer thing — the exile's return to the memorial of grace, and the God who lets his servant take up again the token of an earlier deliverance.
9:
Now the sword is named, and the witnesses treat it as the chapter's richest emblem — a relic at once historical and spiritual. The grammatical-historical commentators secure the facts. Keil and Delitzsch report the priest's answer that the only weapon at hand was the sword of Goliath, “whom David slew in the terebinth valley” (1 Samuel 17:2), wrapped in a cloth behind the ephod — “a sign of the great worth attached to this dedicatory offering” — and that David accepted it as a weapon of unmatched value, since with it he had cut off the giant's head (1 Samuel 17:51). Clarke agrees the sword “was laid up as a trophy in the tabernacle,” and Ellicott adds the vivid conjecture that it lay behind the sacred ephod with the Urim and Thummim, perhaps wrapped in the dead Philistine's own blood-stained war-cloak, for the word rendered “cloth” is used of military attire in Isaiah 9:5.

Ellicott and Wesley together draw out what the blade meant to David's soul. Wesley reads “there is none like that” as more than a soldier's judgment of a good weapon fit for “a strong and tall man”: the sword was “a pledge of God's favour to him,” so that “whenever he looked upon it, it would be a support to his faith, by reminding him of what God had already done.” Ellicott makes the same point with feeling — David “grasped the sword with a childlike expression of joy”; its sight and touch “revived the old bright faith and the sure trust in the strength of Israel” on which he had leaned as a boy against the wild beasts and against the giant, a trust that in these dark days of betrayal “was beginning to fail that gallant spirit.”

So the witnesses, who elsewhere in this chapter must record David's lies and his feigned madness, here pause on a moment of pure recovery. The sword he had once devoted to God in thanksgiving is given back to him in his extremity; what he had dedicated returns to serve and to steady him. Keil and Delitzsch confess they do not know when or how the trophy came to the tabernacle, but all agree on its office now. It is a monument — Wesley's word — of “God's power and goodness,” and the God who let David lay it up in gratitude lets him take it down in need. The lesson the schools leave with is quiet and firm: what is devoted to God's praise is not lost but kept, and in the day of trouble is restored to us for our comfort and defence.
10:
With the sword at his side David crosses the frontier, and the witnesses read his flight to Gath as at once a lapse of faith and a stage of providence. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the desperate logic of it: David fled “that day for fear of Saul” straight out of the land, to Achish of Gath — the Philistine king called Abimelech in the title of Psalm 34, that being “the standing title of the Philistian princes.” So great was the agitation Jonathan's news had stirred in him that he sought refuge among the very Philistines, hoping that after the years since Goliath fell he might pass unrecognized as merely a fugitive from Israel's king. Gill adds that his fear may have been quickened by Doeg, “lest he should go directly to Saul and tell him where he was,” so that David left the same day he came.

The Puritan Matthew Henry lifts the flight into a meditation on the strange ways of God. Here is “David, though king elect… an exile — designed to be master of vast treasures, yet just now begging his bread — anointed to the crown, and yet here forced to flee from his country.” Thus, Henry says, “do God's providences sometimes seem to run counter to his promises, for the trial of his people's faith, and the glorifying of his name.” Israel's darling is driven to quit the land of Israel, and “the Philistine's great enemy” goes to seek shelter among the Philistines — driven out by the king of Israel's personal enmity, and received, for a time, by the king of Gath's personal kindness, who valued his merit.

Henry presses the paradox into a comfort the whole passage yields: “God's persecuted people have often found better usage from Philistines than from Israelites,” as Jeremiah was imprisoned by the king of Judah and set at liberty by the king of Babylon. The witnesses do not conceal that the flight itself sprang from fear — the same weakness of faith that had prompted the lie at Nob — yet they will not let the fear be the last word. The anointed man, hunted from his own inheritance, is not outside the keeping of the God who anointed him; the promise seems to run backward, but only “notwithstanding the difficulties that lay in the way,” and toward the accomplishment of God's counsels. The stage is now set among enemies, where the next verses will find David lower still, and God nearer than he seems.
11:
No sooner is David among the Philistines than he is known, and the witnesses fix on the servants' words and the title they give him. The grammatical-historical commentators explain how the recognition came. Ellicott dismisses the guess that it was Goliath's sword which betrayed him; rather, David “was evidently as well known in Philistia as in his own land,” the folk-song of the Israelite maidens — “Saul hath slain his thousands, and David his ten thousands” — being “current in frontier towns like Gath.” Keil and Delitzsch note the effect on David: he “laid up these words in his heart, and was sore afraid.” Yet Ellicott observes, tellingly, that the Philistines made “no attempt upon his life, or even against his liberty”; the feeling among “his generous foes was rather pitiful admiration mingled with wonder” at seeing so great a doer of splendid deeds reduced to poverty and exile.

The one point of difference concerns the servants' phrase “David the king of the land.” Ellicott takes it loosely — the title “is vaguely used,” for neither the men of Gath nor even David's own countrymen knew of “the sacred anointing by Samuel at Bethlehem” (1 Samuel 16:13); the words mean no more than, Is not this the renowned warrior, the greatest man in Israel of whom the people sing? Wesley reads more into it: they call him king “either for the governor, the most eminent captain,” or “as the king elect, the person designed to be king,” for by now “the fame of Saul's rejection, and David's destination to the kingdom, was got abroad among the Israelites, and from them, probably, to the Philistines.” So the schools weigh the same words toward a lesser or a fuller sense — an honorific for the great captain, or a rumour of the coming crown.

Beneath the difference the witnesses agree on the danger and its irony. Wesley hears in the servants' reminder of the song a veiled counsel to their master: “now our great enemy is in thy hand; be sure thou never let him go alive.” The very fame that had exalted David in Israel now imperils him among Israel's enemies; the ten thousands he had slain rise up in the memory of the men whose champion he had felled. The recognition that should have been his glory becomes his peril, and the witnesses leave him where the verse does — known, praised, and afraid, with the next verse to show what fear will drive him to.
12:
David's fear now becomes the hinge of the story, and the witnesses read his terror with a sober eye. Gill records simply that David “pondered these words in his heart,” turning them over and weighing what the recognition might cost him. The grammatical-historical Ellicott draws the psychological portrait most fully: now “for the first time” David saw how far his renown had travelled, and “at once a deadly fear took possession of him.” Ellicott marks the poignant reversal — “the life he held so cheaply when in battle with the enemies of his country now, strange to say, in his deep degradation and poverty, became of real value to him”; and to save it he “adopted the piteous and humiliating device of feigning madness.”

The Wesleyan Wesley names the double ground of the fear — “lest either their revenge or policy should prompt them to kill him” — and adds the shrewd detail that David “was the more apprehensive because he wore Goliath's sword, which was probably well known at Gath,” the very blade with which he had beheaded their champion now hanging at the side of the man in their power. Out of the experience Wesley draws the lesson David himself would afterward teach: he “now learned by experience” that “it is better to trust in the Lord than to put any confidence in princes” (Psalm 118:9). The favour of Achish, like the favour of Saul before it, was a reed that could not be leaned on.

Ellicott alone reflects on how needless the terror may have been — there is “no reason for supposing that had he maintained a quiet dignity of behaviour any evil would have happened to him,” for the Philistines “seem to have been a cultured people, and by no means devoid of generous instincts.” He notes, too, the strange silence of the whole scene: not a word is spoken of the great injury David had done in slaying Goliath, perhaps out of regard for the dead champion's family, or, as the Talmud moralizes, because “it is wrong to tell the praises of the wicked.” The witnesses do not excuse the panic, which sprang, like the lie at Nob, from a momentary failing of faith; but they let it teach. The man after God's own heart is here most human — afraid, calculating, ready to unmake his own dignity to live — and the God who bore with his fear is the surer refuge his fear forgot.
13:
The device itself now fills the scene, and the witnesses divide over whether the madness was wholly feigned and how far it may be excused. The grammatical-historical commentators describe the act. Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew closely — David “disguised his understanding… behaved insanely under their hands, scribbled upon the door-wings, and let his spittle run down into his beard.” Ellicott glosses each gesture: “he roamed hither and thither, restless and in terror”; “scrabbled” means he scratched upon the doors, though the Septuagint and Vulgate rather have him drumming on them; and he let “the foam which comes from the mouth of a madman… hang about his beard.” Ellicott adds the shrewd observation that David “was only too well acquainted with all the signs of madness, from his long and intimate association with King Saul in his darker hours of insanity” — the hunted man could counterfeit the very disease of the king who hunted him.

Whether the madness was only counterfeit is the verse's one real dispute. Clarke reports that “some imagine David was so terrified… that he was thrown into a kind of frenzy, accompanied with epileptic fits,” a reading the Septuagint seems to favour when it renders the words “Behold, ye see an epileptic man,” and Clarke grants that “the spittle falling upon the beard… is a genuine concomitant of an epileptic fit.” Ellicott records the same suggestion — that “the mind for a time lost its balance” — but concludes with the majority that “the sense of the narrative plainly indicates that the madness was feigned.” Gill takes it as plainly feigned, David having “changed his taste,” acting as if deprived of reason or of speech.

On the morality of the stratagem the Wesleyan Clarke is uneasy. He will not join those who vindicate it: though a Dr. Chandler argues “to deceive the deceiver is… meritorious,” Clarke answers that he can “neither feel the force nor the morality of this,” and warns that “if mocking be catching… he who feigns himself to be mad may, through the just judgment of God, become so.” He dares not “be the apologist of insincerity or lying.” Yet the witnesses temper the censure with pity. Ellicott recalls that these very words open the title of Psalm 34 (which reads Abimelech for Achish), and that other great heroes in mortal peril have feigned madness to escape. The schools thus hold the verse in a double light: the shift is real and cannot be justified as truth, yet it is the desperate contrivance of a cornered man whom God, for all his folly, did not abandon — the low point from which the deliverance of the next verses will lift him.
14:
The trick works, and the witnesses read Achish's exasperated words as the door of David's escape. Gill weighs the king's mind two ways: Achish may have spoken “as willing his servants should think” David mad, the object of pity rather than of malice, or as “really believing he was so” — supposing that Saul's relentless pursuit “had really brought him to distraction,” a conclusion made the likelier by David's coming at all to Gath, “an enemy's country, and whose champion he had slain.” No man in his senses, Achish might think, would have walked into such a place.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds the touch that sharpens the scene: the Philistine king “would look with peculiar sorrow and repulsion on a madman if, as according to Jewish tradition, his own wife and daughter were insane.” However that may be, “the device succeeded, as David hoped it would.” Ellicott supplies the cultural key that makes the escape intelligible: “in old times, as now, in many parts of the East, the insane are looked upon as persons in some peculiar way possessed by, and therefore under the more immediate protection of, Deity.” From the moment the Philistines held David mad, “the life of the hunted fugitive was perfectly safe” — the supposed affliction that shamed him also shielded him.

The witnesses do not divide here; they simply watch the providence turn. The man who had entered Gath armed with Goliath's sword and undone by fear is now let go, not for his strength but for his seeming weakness, driven out because he appeared beneath notice. Ellicott records the wild Talmudic legend that confuses this refuge among the Philistines with a later peril at the hands of Ishbi-benob, the giant's kinsman who bound David crying, “Art thou the man that slew my brother, Goliath?” — a tale that at least remembers how narrow David's margin was. The chapter's irony is complete: the champion is spared because he is thought a fool, and the God who seemed unable to save David's dignity is found saving David's life. Achish's contemptuous question — wherefore have ye brought this man to me? — is, unknown to him, the beginning of David's release.
15:
The chapter ends on Achish's scornful dismissal, and the witnesses read it for its comedy, its providence, and its unresolved moral. Gill dwells on the king's impatience — “Have I need of madmen? do I want them? have not I enough of them already at my court?” — and relays the curious Jewish tradition that “the wife and daughter of Achish were mad,” so that while David “was playing the fool and madman without, they were acting the same part within.” The king had domestic folly enough and needed no more; the great warrior of Israel is waved off as one unwanted jester too many.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the plain sense of the taunt — “shall this fellow come into my house?” — and the Wesleyan Wesley judges shrewdly that “it is highly probable Achish was aware that this madness was counterfeit,” but, “being desirous to preserve David, he speaks as if he thought it real.” On this reading the Philistine king is half in league with the escape, giving his servants a pretext to release a man he had no wish to harm. The witnesses thus leave Achish an ambiguous figure — dupe or discreet protector — but agree that the outcome is David's deliverance.

The Wesleyan Clarke will not let the chapter close without its moral reckoning. He reports Chandler's defence — “he was undone as a wise man, he had a chance to escape as a madman; he tried, and the experiment succeeded” — only to reject it: “Deceit and hypocrisy can never be pleasing in the sight of God.” So the company of witnesses ends its chapter as honestly as Scripture began it, refusing to gild the hero even in his escape. David goes out from Gath alive but not admirable, saved by a shift he ought not to have used, having leaned on lies and feigning where he might have leaned on God. And yet he is saved. The abiding lesson the schools draw from the whole flight is the one David himself would sing out of it — that the fear of man is a snare, and that it is better to trust in the Lord than to put confidence in princes; the God who bore with his servant's weakness in Nob and in Gath was the refuge his weakness kept forgetting, and would not let him fall.
